journal_entry.rationale: Utility services have been consumed but not yet paid, creating an expense and a corresponding liability (accounts payable). Under IFRS accrual accounting, expenses are recognized when incurred, not when cash is paid. The debit tuple (c=1) indicates one expense increase line, and the credit tuple (a=1) indicates one liability increase line. Utilities are not subject to HST/GST as they are typically billed with taxes already included in the stated amount, or the utility company remits taxes directly.

output.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recording an expense that has been incurred but not yet paid. The debit side records the expense increase (utilities expense). This is 1 expense increase line.

debit_classifier.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recording an expense that has been incurred but not yet paid. The debit side records the expense increase (utilities expense). This is 1 expense increase line.

output.reason: Accruing a utility bill creates a liability (accounts payable/accrued expense). The credit side records a liability increase for the $50 owed to the utility company.

credit_classifier.reason: Accruing a utility bill creates a liability (accounts payable/accrued expense). The credit side records a liability increase for the $50 owed to the utility company.

output.reason: Entry correctly accrues $50 utility bill. Debit to Utilities Expense properly records expense incurred, credit to Accounts Payable properly records liability. Entry balances ($50 debit = $50 credit). Amounts match transaction text. Under IFRS accrual accounting, expenses are recognized when incurred regardless of payment timing. No tax issues - utilities are typically invoiced with taxes included or handled by supplier. All necessary lines present.
